---
title: "Discrete Random Variables: The Probability Mass Function"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples / mass
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the CDF / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / cumulative
GREY   = "#6c757d"
STEM   = "#2b3a67"     # pmf stems
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the first of the two concrete kinds of random
> variable, the left fork after [PROB-2 *Random Variables: Turning Outcomes into Numbers*].
> PROB-2 built the random variable as a function $X:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$ and named the one
> object that describes *any* such variable — the cumulative distribution function
> $F(x)=P(X\le x)$. Here we specialize to the case where $X$ lands on a **countable** set
> of values, and we meet the object that makes that case computable: the **probability
> mass function** $p(x)=P(X=x)$. We assume only [PROB-0 *What Is Probability?*] (sample
> spaces and the Kolmogorov axioms) and [PROB-2]. It sets up [PROB-5 *Expectation*] (the
> long-run average is a sum *weighted by the mass*), and the named discrete distributions —
> [PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*] and [PROB-12 *The Poisson
> Distribution*] — which are nothing but particular mass functions. The companion fork is
> [PROB-4 *Continuous Random Variables*], where the continuum forces a density in place of
> a mass.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

When a random variable takes only countably many values — the face of a die, a count of
defects, a number of clicks — its entire probabilistic behavior is captured by one table:
how much **probability mass** sits on each value. This article builds that table, the
**probability mass function** $p(x)=P(X=x)$, directly from the Kolmogorov axioms, proves
the two properties that make a function a legitimate pmf ($p(x)\ge 0$ and $\sum_x p(x)=1$),
and shows how *every* event probability — $P(X\le 3)$, $P(X\text{ is even})$, $P(2\le X<
7)$ — is just a sum of mass over the values the event contains. We then make the pmf and
the CDF of [PROB-2] two views of the same object: the CDF is the running total of the mass
(a sum), and the mass is the size of the CDF's jumps (a difference). The single claim this
article defends is that **for a discrete variable there is nothing more to know than where
the mass sits** — once you have the pmf, the CDF, every probability, and (next article) the
mean and variance are all just bookkeeping on that one table. We carry it through on honest,
*un-named* examples — a loaded die, a casino payout, real customer-arrival counts — so the
machinery is clear before the catalog ever attaches a name to it.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A **discrete** random variable $X$ lands on a countable set of values
$\{x_1,x_2,\dots\}$. Everything about it is encoded in one function, the **probability mass
function**
$$
p(x) = P(X = x),
$$
which records how much probability sits exactly on each value. Two rules make a function a
legitimate pmf — the mass is never negative and it sums to one:
$$
p(x)\ge 0 \quad\text{for all }x, \qquad \sum_{x} p(x) = 1 .
$$
Then the probability of *any* event is a single move: **add up the mass on the values the
event contains**, $P(X\in A)=\sum_{x\in A} p(x)$. The CDF $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ is the running
total of that mass, and the mass is the size of the CDF's jumps — two views of one table.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The phrase "add up the mass" will look almost too simple
to be the whole story, but by the end you should see that for a discrete variable it
genuinely *is* the whole story: there is no integral, no density, no calculus beyond
addition. The subtlety is never in the arithmetic — it is in being honest about *which*
values carry mass and *which* event you are summing over, which is exactly where the
classic mistakes live (@sec-failure).

# Why we are here: the problem the pmf solves {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **once we have turned random outcomes into
numbers, how do we actually compute the probability of anything we care about?** Every step
below is an answer to a piece of that question. If a move feels arbitrary, return here.

## What PROB-2 left us, and what it left undone

[PROB-2] performed the move that lets arithmetic touch chance: it defined a **random
variable** as a function $X:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$ that attaches a number to every outcome in
the sample space $\Omega$ built in [PROB-0]. Roll two dice; the outcome is a pair like
$(3,5)$, but what we care about is the *total*, $X=8$. The variable $X$ is the rule
"add the two faces." That rule turns the messy space of outcomes into plain numbers we can
order, add, and average.

PROB-2 also named the one descriptor that works for *every* random variable — discrete,
continuous, or a hybrid — the **cumulative distribution function**
$$
F(x) = P(X \le x),
$$ {#eq-cdf}
the probability that $X$ comes out at or below the level $x$. The CDF is universal and
powerful, but it is awkward for a variable that lands on separated values. If I ask "what is
the chance the dice total is *exactly* $8$?", the CDF answers only indirectly — I would have
to take $F(8)-F(7^-)$, the height of a jump, and explain what "$7^-$" means. For a discrete
variable there is a far more natural object: a function that just tells me, value by value,
how much probability sits *on* that value. That object is the probability mass function, and
this article is its full story.

## Why "discrete" is the easy, honest case to do first

A random variable is called **discrete** when its possible values form a *countable* set —
either finite ($\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ for one die) or countably infinite ($\{0,1,2,3,\dots\}$ for
a count with no fixed ceiling). "Countable" is the precise word for *listable*: you can put
the values in a sequence $x_1,x_2,x_3,\dots$ and not miss any. (The integers are countable;
the real numbers in $[0,1]$ are not — that is exactly the gap [PROB-4] must cross with a
density, and why this article and that one split.)

The reason discreteness is the right place to start is that it lets us assign probability the
way [PROB-0]'s axioms most naturally allow. Recall **Kolmogorov's axioms** from [PROB-0]:
$P(E)\ge 0$ for every event $E$; $P(\Omega)=1$; and for a sequence of *disjoint* events
$E_1,E_2,\dots$,
$$
P\!\left(\bigcup_{i} E_i\right) = \sum_{i} P(E_i)
\qquad\text{(countable additivity).}
$$ {#eq-additivity}
When $X$ takes countably many values, the events "$X=x_1$", "$X=x_2$", "$\dots$" are
disjoint (a single draw of $X$ cannot equal two different numbers at once) and together they
cover everything $X$ can do. So @eq-additivity says the probabilities on the individual
values are the whole budget, and they must sum to the total mass $P(\Omega)=1$. **The pmf is
nothing more than that budget, written down value by value.** Everything in this article is
a consequence of that one observation.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What exactly makes a random variable discrete — is it that it takes whole-number values?"*
No. Discreteness is about the values being **countable**, not about them being integers. A
payout that takes the values $\{-2.50, 0, 17.00\}$ is discrete (three values, finite). A
variable that takes every value in the interval $[0,1]$ is *not* discrete (that interval is
uncountable), even though every value is a perfectly ordinary number. The dividing line is
listability, and it is the line that decides whether you describe the variable with a mass
function (this article) or a density ([PROB-4]). Some variables are neither pure — a rainfall
amount that is $0$ with positive probability but otherwise spreads over a continuum is a
*mixture*; the CDF of [PROB-2] is what handles all three on equal footing.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formal definition, here is the picture to carry. Imagine you have exactly one
unit — one kilogram, say — of "probability clay," and a number line in front of you. To
describe a discrete random variable, you place little lumps of that clay on the specific
values the variable can take. A fair die: six equal lumps of $1/6$ kg, one on each of
$1$ through $6$. A loaded die: the same six locations, but the lumps are uneven. A coin
counted as $0$/$1$: two lumps. The **mass function** is just the answer to "how heavy is
the lump at value $x$?" — and the iron rule is that **all the clay you have is exactly one
kilogram**, no more, no less. That is the $\sum_x p(x)=1$ in the Core.

Two consequences fall straight out of the clay picture, and they are worth seeing before any
algebra:

- **Probability of a region = total clay in that region.** Want $P(2\le X \le 5)$? Sweep up
  the lumps sitting on $2,3,4,5$ and weigh them. There is no integral to do because the clay
  lives at isolated points — you just add the lump weights. This is the whole computational
  content of a discrete variable.
- **The CDF is a "clay so far" gauge.** Walk left to right along the number line dragging a
  scoop; $F(x)$ is how much clay you have collected by the time you reach $x$. It starts at
  $0$ (no clay to the far left), and it climbs in sudden *jumps* — flat between lumps, then
  stepping up by exactly the lump's weight each time you pass one — until it reaches $1$ once
  every lump is behind you. The jumps *are* the masses.

@fig-pmf-cdf draws both views for a single small variable so the relationship is concrete
before we prove it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pmf-cdf
#| fig-cap: "Two views of one discrete variable. **Left:** the probability mass function — a lump of probability \"clay\" on each value, heights summing to $1$. **Right:** the cumulative distribution function $F(x)=P(X\\le x)$, the running total of that mass. It is a right-continuous step function: flat between values, jumping up by exactly $p(x)$ at each value (jumps marked with the dashed arrows). The pmf is the height of the jumps; the CDF is the running sum of the heights. Filled dots mark the value the step actually takes; open dots mark the limit from the left."
vals = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4])
pmf  = np.array([0.10, 0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.15])
cdf  = np.cumsum(pmf)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))

# --- pmf as a stem plot ---
axL.vlines(vals, 0, pmf, color=STEM, lw=3)
axL.scatter(vals, pmf, color=STEM, s=55, zorder=3)
for v, p in zip(vals, pmf):
    axL.text(v, p + 0.012, f"{p:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=9, color=STEM)
axL.set_ylim(0, 0.37)
axL.set_xlabel("value $x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$p(x)=P(X=x)$")
axL.set_title("Probability mass function")

# --- CDF as a right-continuous step function ---
xs_pad = np.concatenate(([vals[0] - 1], vals, [vals[-1] + 1]))
axR.hlines(0, xs_pad[0], vals[0], color=LINE, lw=2)             # F = 0 to the left
for i, v in enumerate(vals):
    right = vals[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(vals) else xs_pad[-1]
    axR.hlines(cdf[i], v, right, color=LINE, lw=2)              # flat at this level
    axR.scatter([v], [cdf[i]], color=LINE, s=45, zorder=4)      # filled: value taken here
    prev = cdf[i - 1] if i > 0 else 0.0
    axR.scatter([v], [prev], facecolors="white", edgecolors=LINE,
                s=45, zorder=4, linewidths=1.6)                  # open: limit from left
    axR.annotate("", xy=(v, cdf[i]), xytext=(v, prev),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2))
axR.set_ylim(-0.03, 1.05)
axR.set_xlabel("value $x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(x)=P(X\\le x)$")
axR.set_title("Cumulative distribution function")
plt.tight_layout()
```

That figure is the entire article in one image; the rest is making each claim in it precise
and proving it from the axioms.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The probability mass function, defined {#sec-def-pmf}

::: {#def-pmf}
**Definition (probability mass function).** Let $X$ be a discrete random variable taking
values in a countable set $\mathcal{X}=\{x_1,x_2,\dots\}\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. Its
**probability mass function** (pmf) is the function $p:\mathbb{R}\to[0,1]$ defined by
$$
p(x) = P(X = x) ,
$$ {#eq-pmf-def}
the probability that $X$ takes the exact value $x$. For values $x$ that $X$ cannot take, the
event $\{X=x\}$ is empty, so $p(x)=P(\varnothing)=0$ by [PROB-0].
:::

Unpack every symbol in @eq-pmf-def, because each earns its place. The set $\mathcal{X}$ is
the **support** of $X$ — the values that actually carry positive mass (made precise in
@sec-support). The event "$X=x$" is shorthand for the set of outcomes $\omega\in\Omega$ for
which the function $X$ returns the number $x$; formally $\{X=x\}=\{\omega\in\Omega: X(\omega)=x\}$,
which is a genuine event in the sense of [PROB-0], so $P$ is allowed to act on it. The pmf is
defined for *every* real $x$, but it is zero everywhere except on the countable support — the
clay sits only at the lumps.

A point of notation we will hold to: lowercase $x$ is a *particular number* (a value the
variable might equal), while uppercase $X$ is the *random variable* itself (the rule, not yet
evaluated). The statement $P(X=x)$ reads "the probability that the random thing $X$ comes out
equal to the fixed number $x$." When several variables are in play we write $p_X(x)$ to mark
*whose* mass function it is.

## The two defining properties — derived, not declared {#sec-properties}

The Core asserts that a pmf satisfies $p(x)\ge 0$ and $\sum_x p(x)=1$. We do not declare
these; we derive them from the Kolmogorov axioms of [PROB-0], because that is where their
authority comes from.

::: {#thm-pmf-props}
**Theorem (the pmf axioms).** For any discrete random variable $X$ with pmf $p$,
$$
\textbf{(i)}\quad p(x)\ge 0 \text{ for every } x\in\mathbb{R},
\qquad\qquad
\textbf{(ii)}\quad \sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}} p(x) = 1 ,
$$
where the sum runs over the support $\mathcal{X}$. Conversely, any function $p$ satisfying
(i) and (ii) on a countable set is the pmf of *some* discrete random variable.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Property (i).** By the first Kolmogorov axiom, *every* event has probability at least zero.
The set $\{X=x\}$ is an event, so $p(x)=P(X=x)\ge 0$. That is the whole argument — non-negativity
of mass is just non-negativity of probability, inherited unchanged.

**Property (ii).** This is where countable additivity does the work. List the support as
$\mathcal{X}=\{x_1,x_2,\dots\}$. Consider the events
$$
E_i = \{X = x_i\}, \qquad i = 1,2,\dots
$$
These events are **pairwise disjoint**: if $\omega\in E_i\cap E_j$ with $i\ne j$, then
$X(\omega)=x_i$ *and* $X(\omega)=x_j$, forcing $x_i=x_j$, a contradiction since the listed
values are distinct. A single evaluation of $X$ returns one number, so it cannot belong to
two different "$X=x_i$" events at once.

These events are also **exhaustive**: every outcome $\omega\in\Omega$ produces *some* value
$X(\omega)$, and since $X$ is discrete that value lies in $\mathcal{X}$, so $\omega$ belongs
to exactly one $E_i$. Therefore their union is the entire sample space,
$$
\bigcup_{i} E_i = \Omega .
$$
Now apply countable additivity @eq-additivity to this disjoint union, and then the second
axiom $P(\Omega)=1$:
$$
\sum_{i} p(x_i)
= \sum_{i} P(E_i)
\;\overset{\text{@eq-additivity}}{=}\;
P\!\left(\bigcup_{i} E_i\right)
= P(\Omega) = 1 .
$$
That is exactly $\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}} p(x)=1$. The total mass is one because the values
partition the sample space and probability is countably additive across a partition.

**Converse.** Suppose $p\ge 0$ on a countable set $\{x_1,x_2,\dots\}$ with $\sum_i p(x_i)=1$.
Take $\Omega=\{x_1,x_2,\dots\}$ itself, define $P(\{x_i\})=p(x_i)$ and extend to any subset by
summing (which satisfies the axioms precisely because $p\ge0$ and the total is $1$), and let
$X$ be the identity map $X(x_i)=x_i$. Then $P(X=x_i)=p(x_i)$, so $p$ is realized as a genuine
pmf. This is the licence to *invent* a discrete variable just by writing down a valid mass
table — which is exactly what we do for the loaded die and the payout below. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The converse is not a technicality; it is the working permission for the rest of the track.
When [PROB-11] writes down the binomial mass $p(k)=\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$, the only thing
it must check to be allowed to call it a distribution is that those numbers are non-negative
and sum to one. @thm-pmf-props says that check is *sufficient* — nothing else is required.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Can a probability mass ever exceed $1$?"* No — and the reason is property (i)–(ii) together,
not a separate rule. Each $p(x)$ is one term of a sum of non-negative numbers that totals
exactly $1$, so no single term can exceed the total: $0\le p(x)\le 1$. Watch this carefully,
because it is **the cleanest contrast with the continuous case**: a probability *density*
([PROB-4]) routinely exceeds $1$, since a density is mass *per unit length*, not mass. A mass
is a probability and is capped at $1$; a density is not a probability and is not capped. Mixing
the two up is the single most common conceptual error when crossing from this article to the
next.
:::

## The support {#sec-support}

::: {#def-support}
**Definition (support).** The **support** of $X$ is the set of values carrying positive mass,
$$
\mathcal{X} = \{\, x \in \mathbb{R} : p(x) > 0 \,\}.
$$
:::

The support is the honest answer to "what can this variable actually do?" Everything outside
it has $p(x)=0$ and may be ignored in every sum — including it changes nothing, since adding
zeros to a sum is free. We will routinely write $\sum_x$ to mean $\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}$,
because the zero terms are harmless. The support can be finite (a die: $\{1,\dots,6\}$) or
countably infinite (a Poisson count: $\{0,1,2,\dots\}$). When it is infinite, property (ii)
becomes a statement about an *infinite series* converging to $1$ — a real constraint we
exploit in @sec-geometric.

## Event probabilities are sums over the mass {#sec-events}

Here is the payoff property — the one that makes the pmf worth defining. To get the
probability of *any* event described in terms of $X$, you add up the mass on the values that
event allows.

::: {#thm-event}
**Theorem (event probability as a sum).** For any set $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$,
$$
P(X\in A) = \sum_{x\in A} p(x) ,
$$ {#eq-event-sum}
where the sum runs over the values in $A$ that lie in the support (the rest contribute $0$).
:::

::: {.proof}
The event $\{X\in A\}$ is the set of outcomes for which $X$ lands in $A$. Decompose it by
*which* value of the support $X$ hits:
$$
\{X\in A\} = \bigcup_{x\in A\cap\mathcal{X}} \{X = x\} .
$$
This is a union of the single-value events $\{X=x\}$, taken over the supported values inside
$A$. As established in the proof of @thm-pmf-props, distinct single-value events are pairwise
disjoint — a draw of $X$ equals only one number — so this is a disjoint union of a countable
collection. Countable additivity @eq-additivity applies term by term:
$$
P(X\in A) = \sum_{x\in A\cap\mathcal{X}} P(X=x) = \sum_{x\in A\cap\mathcal{X}} p(x)
= \sum_{x\in A} p(x),
$$
the last equality because values of $A$ outside the support contribute $p(x)=0$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@eq-event-sum is the engine of every discrete probability calculation. Three immediate
specializations cover almost everything you will ever compute:

- **An interval / range:** $P(a \le X \le b) = \sum_{x:\, a\le x\le b} p(x)$. Just sweep up the
  mass between the endpoints. Mind whether the endpoints are *included* — that is where the
  off-by-one mistakes live (@sec-failure).
- **A condition:** $P(X\text{ is even}) = \sum_{x\text{ even}} p(x)$, $P(X > 3)=\sum_{x>3}p(x)$.
  Any rule that selects values selects the masses to add.
- **The complement shortcut:** $P(X \ge 4) = 1 - P(X\le 3)$, because the two events partition
  the line and the total mass is $1$. Often the complement is the shorter sum — a habit worth
  forming.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is $P(a\le X\le b)$ a sum here, but an integral for a continuous variable?"* Because the
mass lives at *isolated points*. Probability "in a region" means "the probability assigned to
the region," and for a discrete variable the region's probability is the sum of the point
masses inside it — there is no width to integrate over, the clay sits at the lumps. In [PROB-4]
the probability is smeared continuously, so the analogous total is $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. The
$\sum$ here and the $\int$ there are the *same idea* — total the probability in the region —
under two different ways probability can be distributed. Seeing them as one idea, not two
formulas, is what an interviewer is probing.
:::

## The CDF is the running sum; the pmf is the jump {#sec-cdf-pmf}

We now make precise the right-hand picture of @fig-pmf-cdf: the pmf and the CDF of [PROB-2]
are two views of one table, related by summing in one direction and differencing in the other.

**From pmf to CDF (sum).** Apply @eq-event-sum with $A=(-\infty, x]$:
$$
F(x) = P(X \le x) = \sum_{t\,\le\, x} p(t) ,
$$ {#eq-cdf-from-pmf}
the running total of all mass at or below $x$. As $x$ increases past a supported value, the
sum gains that value's mass; between supported values, no new mass is added and $F$ stays
flat. So $F$ is a **step function**: flat stretches punctuated by upward jumps. It is
**right-continuous** — at a supported value $x_0$, the sum @eq-cdf-from-pmf already *includes*
$p(x_0)$ (the inequality is $t\le x$, not $t<x$), so $F$ takes the post-jump value *at* $x_0$;
this is exactly the filled-dot/open-dot convention in @fig-pmf-cdf. These are the three
defining CDF properties from [PROB-2] — monotone, right-continuous, limits $0$ and $1$ —
specialized to a step function.

**From CDF to pmf (difference).** The mass at $x_0$ is the *height of the jump* there. Writing
$F(x_0^-)=\lim_{x\uparrow x_0}F(x)$ for the limit approached from the left (the height *just
before* the jump),
$$
p(x_0) = F(x_0) - F(x_0^-) .
$$ {#eq-pmf-from-cdf}
This is the inverse of @eq-cdf-from-pmf: where summing built the steps, differencing recovers
the step heights.

::: {#lem-jump}
**Lemma (the jump is the mass).** For any $x_0$, $\;F(x_0)-F(x_0^-)=P(X=x_0)=p(x_0)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By @eq-cdf-from-pmf, $F(x_0)=\sum_{t\le x_0}p(t)$. The left limit $F(x_0^-)$ collects all mass
*strictly* below $x_0$: as $x$ rises toward $x_0$ without reaching it, the sum
$\sum_{t\le x}p(t)$ includes every supported value below $x_0$ but not $x_0$ itself, and in
the limit it equals $\sum_{t< x_0}p(t)$. (More carefully: for $x$ between $x_0$ and the next
supported value below it, $\sum_{t\le x}p(t)=\sum_{t<x_0}p(t)$ exactly, since no supported
value lies in that gap; so the left limit *is* that constant value.) Subtracting,
$$
F(x_0)-F(x_0^-) = \sum_{t\le x_0}p(t) - \sum_{t< x_0}p(t) = p(x_0),
$$
because the two sums differ in exactly the single term $t=x_0$. And $p(x_0)=P(X=x_0)$ by
@def-pmf. So the jump height equals the point mass. Where $x_0$ carries no mass, $p(x_0)=0$ and
$F$ has no jump — it is continuous there. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@lem-jump is more than bookkeeping: it is the reason discreteness is *visible* in the CDF.
A discrete variable's CDF is a pure staircase (all of its rise happens in jumps); a continuous
variable's CDF rises smoothly with no jumps (every $P(X=x)=0$); a mixture has both. The shape
of $F$ tells you which kind of variable you have, which is precisely the diagnostic [PROB-2]
promised and the seam between this article and [PROB-4].

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Given a CDF, how do you recover $P(X=3)$, $P(X<3)$, and $P(X\le 3)$ — and why are they
different?"* Use the staircase. $P(X\le 3)=F(3)$ reads the step *at* $3$ (right-continuous, so
$F(3)$ already includes the mass at $3$). $P(X<3)=F(3^-)$ reads the height *just before* the
jump at $3$ — it *excludes* the mass at $3$. And $P(X=3)=F(3)-F(3^-)$ is the jump itself, the
difference of the two. They differ by exactly $p(3)$. For a *continuous* variable ([PROB-4])
the point mass is $p(3)=P(X=3)=0$, so the jump height is zero and $F(3^-)=F(3)$; substituting
that into the three expressions gives $P(X<3)=F(3^-)=F(3)=P(X\le 3)$ and $P(X=3)=F(3)-F(3^-)=0$
— so all three collapse to the same number. That is precisely why the strict-vs-weak inequality
matters here, where $p(3)>0$, but not there, where it is $0$. Getting this triple right is a
standard exam discriminator.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on three honest examples — none of them a *named* distribution yet,
so you watch the pmf, CDF, and event-sums work as raw definitions before [PROB-11]/[PROB-12]
ever attach a name. The first we carry through entirely by hand.

## Example 1 — a loaded die {#sec-loaded-die}

A casino's six-sided die is suspected of being shaved so that high faces come up more often.
Independent testing produced the following mass function for the face $X\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$.
This is a pmf we simply *declare* — the converse half of @thm-pmf-props licenses it, provided
the numbers are non-negative and sum to one, which we verify first.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-die
faces = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])
p_die = np.array([0.10, 0.12, 0.13, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30])
print("face x:    ", "  ".join(f"{f:>4d}" for f in faces))
print("p(x):      ", "  ".join(f"{p:>4.2f}" for p in p_die))
print(f"\n(i)  all p(x) >= 0 ? {np.all(p_die >= 0)}")
print(f"(ii) sum p(x)     = {p_die.sum():.2f}   (must be 1)")
```

Both pmf axioms hold, so this is a legitimate discrete variable. Now compute three event
probabilities *by hand*, each a direct application of @eq-event-sum — adding the mass on the
allowed faces.

**The chance of rolling at least a $5$.** The event $A=\{X\ge 5\}$ contains the faces $5$ and
$6$, so we sum their mass:
$$
P(X\ge 5) = p(5) + p(6) = 0.20 + 0.30 = 0.50 .
$$
Half the time this die shows a $5$ or $6$ — a fair die would do that only a third of the time,
which is exactly the shaving the casino feared.

**The chance of an even face.** The event $\{X\text{ even}\}=\{2,4,6\}$:
$$
P(X\text{ even}) = p(2)+p(4)+p(6) = 0.12 + 0.15 + 0.30 = 0.57 .
$$

**A range, the long way and the short way.** For $P(2\le X\le 4)$ we add directly,
$$
P(2\le X\le 4) = p(2)+p(3)+p(4) = 0.12+0.13+0.15 = 0.40 ,
$$
or use the complement shortcut from @sec-events: $P(2\le X\le 4)=F(4)-F(1)$, where
$F(4)=p(1)+\dots+p(4)=0.50$ and $F(1)=0.10$, giving $0.50-0.10=0.40$ — the same answer,
confirming @eq-cdf-from-pmf. @fig-die shows the pmf and its staircase CDF, with the "$X\ge 5$"
event shaded.

```{python}
#| label: fig-die
#| fig-cap: "The loaded die. **Left:** the pmf, with the event $\\{X\\ge 5\\}$ shaded — its total height ($0.20+0.30=0.50$) is $P(X\\ge 5)$ by @eq-event-sum. **Right:** the CDF as a staircase; each jump height equals the corresponding mass (@lem-jump), and the total rise is exactly $1$. The fair-die mass $1/6\\approx0.167$ is drawn as a dashed reference — the die's mass is clearly tilted toward the high faces."
cdf_die = np.cumsum(p_die)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))

# pmf with the {X>=5} event shaded
colors = [ACCENT if f >= 5 else STEM for f in faces]
axL.vlines(faces, 0, p_die, color=colors, lw=4)
axL.scatter(faces, p_die, color=colors, s=55, zorder=3)
axL.axhline(1/6, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3, label="fair die $1/6$")
for f, p in zip(faces, p_die):
    axL.text(f, p + 0.008, f"{p:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=8.5, color=STEM)
axL.set_ylim(0, 0.34)
axL.set_xlabel("face $x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$p(x)$")
axL.set_title("pmf (shaded: $X\\ge 5$)"); axL.legend(fontsize=9, loc="upper left")

# CDF staircase
axR.hlines(0, 0, faces[0], color=LINE, lw=2)
for i, f in enumerate(faces):
    right = faces[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(faces) else 7
    axR.hlines(cdf_die[i], f, right, color=LINE, lw=2)
    axR.scatter([f], [cdf_die[i]], color=LINE, s=40, zorder=4)
    prev = cdf_die[i - 1] if i > 0 else 0.0
    axR.scatter([f], [prev], facecolors="white", edgecolors=LINE, s=40, zorder=4, lw=1.5)
axR.set_ylim(-0.03, 1.05)
axR.set_xlabel("face $x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(x)$")
axR.set_title("CDF (staircase; jumps = masses)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — a casino game payout {#sec-payout}

Now a variable whose values are not the outcomes themselves but a *function* of them — the
single most useful move in the whole subject, because it is what turns "what happened" into
"what it cost me." A small game: pay \$1 to roll the *fair* die above's honest cousin (a fair
six-sided die). You win \$5 on a $6$, win \$1 on a $5$, and otherwise win nothing. Let $X$ be
your **net profit** in dollars (winnings minus the \$1 stake). Then $X$ is a discrete variable
on three values, and we build its pmf from the die's pmf by the rule from [PROB-2]: the mass
of a value of $X$ is the total mass of the outcomes that *map to* it.

The fair die has $p(\text{face})=1/6$ each. Mapping faces to net profit:

- A $6$ pays \$5, net $\$5-\$1 = \$4$. Only the face $6$ does this: mass $1/6$.
- A $5$ pays \$1, net $\$1-\$1 = \$0$. Only the face $5$: mass $1/6$.
- Faces $1,2,3,4$ pay \$0, net $\$0-\$1 = -\$1$. Four faces: mass $4/6 = 2/3$.

So the net-profit variable $X$ has support $\{-1, 0, 4\}$ with the pmf below. This is
@eq-event-sum used in reverse: each profit value's mass is the *sum* of the masses of the
faces producing it.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-payout
prof   = np.array([-1, 0, 4])
p_prof = np.array([4/6, 1/6, 1/6])
print("net profit x:  ", "   ".join(f"{v:>3d}" for v in prof))
print("p(x):          ", "   ".join(f"{p:>4.3f}" for p in p_prof))
print(f"\n(i)  all p(x) >= 0 ? {np.all(p_prof >= 0)}")
print(f"(ii) sum p(x)     = {p_prof.sum():.3f}")
print(f"\nP(profit >= 0) = p(0) + p(4) = {p_prof[1] + p_prof[2]:.3f}")
print(f"P(loss)        = p(-1)        = {p_prof[0]:.3f}")
```

The mass on $-1$ is the heaviest: you lose your stake two-thirds of the time, and the rare \$4
win on a $6$ is what the game dangles to keep you playing. We are *not* yet computing whether
the game is worth playing on average — that is **expectation**, $E[X]=\sum_x x\,p(x)$, the
weighted sum of the values against this very mass, and it is the entire subject of [PROB-5].
The point here is narrower and prior: the pmf of a transformed variable is built by *pooling
the mass* of the outcomes that land on each new value. @fig-payout draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-payout
#| fig-cap: "The casino game's net-profit pmf. Each bar's height is the pooled mass of the die faces that produce that profit: four faces collapse onto a \\$1 loss (mass $2/3$), one face each onto break-even and a \\$4 win (mass $1/6$). The heavy mass on the loss is the house edge made visible — turning this picture into a single \"is it worth it?\" number is the job of expectation in [PROB-5]."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 3.8))
bars = ax.bar(prof, p_prof, width=0.4, color=[LINE, GREY, ACCENT])
for v, p in zip(prof, p_prof):
    ax.text(v, p + 0.012, f"{p:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xticks(prof)
ax.set_xticklabels(["$-\\$1$ (loss)", "$\\$0$ (even)", "$+\\$4$ (win)"])
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.75)
ax.set_ylabel("$p(x)$"); ax.set_xlabel("net profit $x$")
ax.set_title("pmf of net profit (fair-die game)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — real arrival counts, and the edge of the finite case {#sec-geometric}

The first two examples had finite support. Many real discrete variables do not: a *count* —
support tickets in an hour, photons hitting a detector, customers through a door — has no
fixed ceiling, so its support is the countably infinite set $\{0,1,2,\dots\}$. We take a real
operational dataset and confront the one genuinely new wrinkle: property (ii) now demands an
**infinite series** sum to $1$.

We use hourly counts of arriving customers at a small store, recorded over many hours. The
**empirical pmf** — the fraction of hours showing exactly $k$ arrivals — is itself a perfectly
valid mass function (non-negative, sums to one), and it is the data's own estimate of the
underlying mass. (That this empirical object converges to the truth as data accumulate is the
content of the *empirical CDF* in [PROB-22]; here we just use it as a real, honest pmf.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-arrivals
#| fig-cap: "Real-style hourly arrival counts ($1{,}500$ hours). **Left:** the empirical pmf — the fraction of hours with exactly $k$ arrivals — overlaid (red dots) with a fitted geometric-tail mass $p(k)=(1-r)r^{k}$ for comparison. **Right:** the empirical CDF as a staircase climbing toward $1$ but never quite finished — the visible signature of *infinite support*. The tail mass beyond the plotted range is small but genuinely nonzero, which is exactly why property (ii) is a statement about a convergent series, not a finite sum."
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
# A realistic right-skewed count with a long thin tail (no fixed ceiling).
counts = rng.geometric(p=0.28, size=1500) - 1   # support {0,1,2,...}
kmax = 14
ks = np.arange(0, kmax + 1)
emp = np.array([(counts == k).mean() for k in ks])

# A geometric mass p(k) = (1-r) r^k fitted by matching the mean.
mean_hat = counts.mean()
r = mean_hat / (1 + mean_hat)          # mean of geometric-on-{0,1,..} is r/(1-r)
geom = (1 - r) * r**ks

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.vlines(ks, 0, emp, color=STEM, lw=3)
axL.scatter(ks, emp, color=STEM, s=30, zorder=3, label="empirical $p(k)$")
axL.plot(ks, geom, "o", color=LINE, ms=4, label=r"geometric $(1-r)r^k$")
axL.set_xlabel("arrivals in an hour, $k$"); axL.set_ylabel("$p(k)$")
axL.set_title("Empirical pmf of arrivals"); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

emp_cdf = np.cumsum(emp)
axR.hlines(0, -1, ks[0], color=LINE, lw=2)
for i, k in enumerate(ks):
    right = ks[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(ks) else kmax + 1
    axR.hlines(emp_cdf[i], k, right, color=LINE, lw=2)
    axR.scatter([k], [emp_cdf[i]], color=LINE, s=22, zorder=4)
axR.axhline(1.0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.set_ylim(-0.03, 1.05)
axR.set_xlabel("arrivals $k$"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(k)$")
axR.set_title("Empirical CDF (climbing toward 1)")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"sample mean arrivals/hour = {mean_hat:.3f}")
print(f"empirical mass on k=0..14 = {emp.sum():.4f}  (the rest is in the tail)")
```

The empirical pmf sums to almost — but not exactly — one over the plotted range, with a sliver
of mass out in the tail. That is the honest face of infinite support. To make the point with a
clean closed form, fit the overlaid **geometric** mass $p(k)=(1-r)r^k$ for $k=0,1,2,\dots$
(this is a *named* distribution we are borrowing early; [PROB-11] builds it properly). Watch
property (ii) become a convergence statement, and watch us *prove* it converges to one rather
than asserting it.

We need the sum $\sum_{k=0}^\infty (1-r) r^k$ to equal $1$, which requires the **geometric
series** identity. We do not assume it — we derive it from Calc-1 algebra. Let $0<r<1$ and
consider the partial sum of the first $n+1$ terms,
$$
S_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} r^k = 1 + r + r^2 + \dots + r^n .
$$
Multiply through by $r$ and subtract — the classic telescoping trick:
$$
S_n - r S_n = (1 + r + \dots + r^n) - (r + r^2 + \dots + r^{n+1}) = 1 - r^{\,n+1},
$$
since every interior term cancels with its shifted copy, leaving only the first term of the
first sum and the last term of the second. Factor the left side, $S_n(1-r)=1-r^{n+1}$, and
divide by $1-r$ (nonzero, as $r\ne 1$):
$$
S_n = \frac{1 - r^{\,n+1}}{1 - r}.
$$
Now take the limit as $n\to\infty$. Because $0<r<1$, the power $r^{\,n+1}\to 0$ (raising a
number below one to ever-higher powers drives it to zero — a Calc-1 limit), so
$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k = \lim_{n\to\infty} S_n = \frac{1 - 0}{1-r} = \frac{1}{1-r} .
$$ {#eq-geometric}
Multiply by the constant $(1-r)$ out front of our mass:
$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (1-r) r^k = (1-r)\cdot\frac{1}{1-r} = 1 .
$$
Property (ii) holds — the total mass is one — *and we have proved the series converges*, not
taken it on faith. The same identity will return repeatedly in the catalog (it is the
backbone of the geometric and negative-binomial distributions in [PROB-11]).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"With infinite support, how can the probabilities possibly add to one?"* Because the masses
shrink geometrically (or faster) and a convergent infinite series *can* total a finite value —
here exactly $1$, by @eq-geometric. The intuition: each value gets a positive sliver of mass,
but the slivers vanish fast enough that the whole infinite collection still weighs one
kilogram. The requirement is not "finitely many values" but "the series converges to one." A
function that put **equal** positive mass on every one of the infinitely many integers would
fail, and here is the actual argument — not just the clay picture. Suppose $p(x)=c$ for every
integer $x$ in an infinite support, with $c$ the same constant everywhere. There are only two
cases. If $c>0$, then property (ii) demands $\sum_x p(x)=\sum_x c$, and adding a fixed positive
number to itself infinitely often diverges — the partial sums $c, 2c, 3c, \dots$ grow without
bound and never settle on the finite value $1$, so the series cannot equal $1$. If $c=0$, then
every term is $0$ and the sum is $0\ne 1$, so property (ii) fails the other way. No constant
$c$ works, so **a *uniform* distribution on a countably infinite set does not exist** — the very
thing that forces the mass to *decay* (geometrically here, or faster) rather than stay flat.
This is itself a clean interview trap.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The pmf is almost foolproof to *use* — it is addition. The mistakes are never arithmetic; they
are about *which* mass you are summing and *whether* a mass function is the right object at all.
This section is what separates "can add up a table" from "knows what the table means."

## The strict-vs-weak inequality off-by-one {#sec-offbyone}

The single most common error in discrete probability is confusing $P(X\le k)$ with $P(X<k)$,
or $P(X\ge k)$ with $P(X>k)$. For a *continuous* variable these are interchangeable (the
single point carries zero mass), so habits formed there transfer wrongly. For a discrete
variable they differ by exactly the endpoint mass $p(k)$, which is generally not zero.

```{python}
#| label: fig-offbyone
#| fig-cap: "Why the inequality direction matters for a discrete variable. Using the loaded die: $P(X\\le 4)=0.50$ (shaded, *includes* the mass at $4$) versus $P(X<4)=0.35$ (excludes it). The two differ by exactly $p(4)=0.15$ — the boxed mass. For a continuous variable this gap vanishes; here it does not, and treating $\\le$ and $<$ as the same is the classic off-by-one bug."
F4   = p_die[:4].sum()    # P(X <= 4)
Flt4 = p_die[:3].sum()    # P(X <  4)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.8))
incl = faces <= 4
ax.vlines(faces, 0, p_die, color=[ACCENT if i else GREY for i in incl], lw=4)
ax.scatter(faces, p_die, color=[ACCENT if i else GREY for i in incl], s=50, zorder=3)
# highlight the disputed endpoint mass at x=4
ax.vlines(4, 0, p_die[3], color=LINE, lw=4)
ax.scatter([4], [p_die[3]], color=LINE, s=70, zorder=4)
ax.text(4, p_die[3] + 0.01, f"$p(4)={p_die[3]:.2f}$\n(in $\\le$, out of $<$)",
        ha="center", fontsize=9, color=LINE)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.36)
ax.set_xlabel("face $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$p(x)$")
ax.set_title(f"$P(X\\le 4)={F4:.2f}$  vs  $P(X<4)={Flt4:.2f}$  (differ by $p(4)$)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The fix is mechanical but must be deliberate: decide whether the boundary value is *in* the
event, and reach for the matching CDF expression — $P(X\le k)=F(k)$, $P(X<k)=F(k^-)=F(k-1)$ on
the integers, $P(X\ge k)=1-F(k-1)$, $P(X>k)=1-F(k)$. Writing these out on integer support is
worth doing once until it is reflex.

## Mass on the wrong space, or that does not sum to one {#sec-invalid}

A "pmf" that does not satisfy @thm-pmf-props is not a pmf, and two corruptions are common in
practice. **Negative mass** sneaks in when someone computes "probabilities" by an unconstrained
fit or a subtraction and forgets the $p\ge0$ axiom — the result is meaningless, since a negative
probability has no interpretation under [PROB-0]. **Mass that does not total one** appears when
a sum is truncated (the arrivals tail of @sec-geometric, dropped) or double-counts an outcome;
the cure is **renormalization** — divide every mass by the actual total $Z=\sum_x \tilde p(x)$
so that $p(x)=\tilde p(x)/Z$ sums to one. Renormalization is legitimate *only* when the
unnormalized $\tilde p$ is already non-negative; you cannot rescale away a sign error.

## When a pmf is the wrong object entirely {#sec-wrong-object}

The honest boundary: the pmf describes a variable whose probability lives *at points*. It is the
wrong tool — not just inconvenient, but undefined in the form $P(X=x)$ — when the variable is
**continuous**. For a continuous variable, $P(X=x)=0$ for *every* single value (there are
uncountably many values to share one unit of mass, so no individual point can hold a positive
sliver), and a mass function would be identically zero, telling you nothing. That is precisely
why [PROB-4] replaces the mass with a **density** $f(x)$ and the sum with an integral. The tell
is in the CDF: if $F$ rises *smoothly* with no jumps, there is no mass to tabulate and you need a
density; if $F$ is a staircase, the pmf is exactly right; if it has both jumps and smooth rises,
the variable is a **mixture** and needs both. Trying to force a pmf onto a continuous variable —
or a density onto a discrete one — is the deepest version of using the wrong object.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A probability mass is a probability; a probability density is not.** A mass $p(x)$ is capped
at $1$ and is read off directly as $P(X=x)$. A density $f(x)$ ([PROB-4]) can exceed $1$ and is
*not* a probability — only its integral over a region is. The symptoms look similar (both are
non-negative, both "integrate/sum to one"), so the trap is to carry pmf intuition into the
continuous world. The discriminator is always the same: discrete mass lives at points and you
*sum*; continuous density spreads over intervals and you *integrate*. Decide which world you are
in *before* you compute, by looking at whether the CDF jumps or is smooth.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When should you model something with a discrete distribution, and when not?"* The honest
summary:

- **Use a pmf** when the variable is intrinsically a **count or a category coded as numbers** —
  successes in $n$ trials, events in a window, the face of a die, the number of defects, a
  net payout taking finitely many values. The values are listable and probability sits on them.
- **Do not use a pmf** for a genuinely continuous quantity — a time, a length, a temperature,
  a measured concentration — where every exact value has probability zero; reach for a *density*
  ([PROB-4]). Beware the common *near*-discrete case: data recorded to two decimals *looks*
  discrete but is a rounded continuous quantity, and is usually better modeled continuously.
- **Watch for mixtures** — a quantity that is exactly zero with positive probability and
  otherwise continuous (rainfall, insurance claims, time-to-event with a spike at zero). Neither
  a pure pmf nor a pure density fits; the CDF of [PROB-2] is the object that handles all three,
  and you model the discrete spike and continuous part separately.

The deepest version of the question is just "does probability sit at points or spread over a
continuum?" — and the CDF's jumps-vs-smoothness answers it.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-2 *Random Variables*]**, which made $X$ a function on the sample space
and named the universal descriptor $F(x)=P(X\le x)$. This article specialized to the
**countable-valued** case and built its natural descriptor, the **probability mass function**
$p(x)=P(X=x)$ — proving its two axioms from [PROB-0]'s countable additivity, showing every event
probability is a sum of mass (@eq-event-sum), and tying the pmf to the CDF as jumps-and-running-
sums (@lem-jump). From here the trunk continues:

- **[PROB-4 *Continuous Random Variables*]** — the companion fork: when the values fill a
  continuum, $P(X=x)=0$ and the mass becomes a **density** $f(x)$, the sum an **integral**. The
  jumps-vs-smoothness of the CDF is the seam between the two articles.
- **[PROB-5 *Expectation*]** — the long-run average $E[X]=\sum_x x\,p(x)$ is the values weighted
  by *this very mass*; the casino payout of @sec-payout is waiting for exactly this number to
  decide whether the game is worth playing. Variance ([PROB-6]) is the next weighted sum.
- **[PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*]** and **[PROB-12 *The Poisson
  Distribution*]** — the named discrete distributions are nothing but particular mass functions;
  every one of them must pass the @thm-pmf-props check we built here (the geometric series of
  @sec-geometric is the engine that proves several of them sum to one). The counting machinery
  their masses need is **[PROB-10]**.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The historical thread.** The mass-function view of discrete chance traces to the earliest
  probability — Cardano's dice tallies (16th c.), Pascal and Fermat's 1654 correspondence on the
  problem of points, and Jacob Bernoulli's *Ars Conjectandi* (1713), where counting the favorable
  arrangements *is* assigning mass. The modern footing — mass as a special case of a measure on a
  countable set, justified by countable additivity — is Kolmogorov's 1933 *Grundbegriffe*, the
  same source as [PROB-0]'s axioms.
- **The unifying view this article deliberately deferred.** The pmf (sum) and the pdf (integral,
  [PROB-4]) are two faces of one object: a *probability measure*, integrated against a *counting
  measure* in the discrete case and against *Lebesgue measure* in the continuous case. That
  unification is measure-theoretic and lives above this track's floor; we flag it here so you know
  the $\sum$/$\int$ duality is not a coincidence but a single integral in disguise.
- **Open threads.** *What single number summarizes where the mass sits?* — the mean, [PROB-5].
  *How spread out is it?* — the variance, [PROB-6]. *What if two discrete variables vary together?*
  — the joint pmf, [PROB-7]. *Where do real count distributions come from?* — the catalog,
  [PROB-11]–[PROB-12], with the negative-binomial as the honest fix when a Poisson count is
  over-dispersed.
